Thank you everybody. Today I decided just to kind of throw things open and answer the questions that you guys are thinking about and see if we can't help you with some pretty specific advice to get things going. Let's get started. You guys were great. The number of questions that came in were just amazing and the intelligence behind the questions really, really impressed me. It's clear that you guys are serious about what you're doing and that's really encouraging to us. So let's dive right in. The first question had to do with an approach to pricing your services for copywriters from Cheryl. On another mentor's blog he recommended a menu offering a menu of services. Kind of like when you go to the gas station there's regular and premium. What are they? My portion only takes premium. Regular mid-grade and premium gasoline. Or if you go to the Olympics you've got bronze, silver and gold medals. Cheryl read this blog and kind of liked the idea of creating more or less a bronze, silver and gold level of service for her clients so that they can select just the services that they want from her and not pay for what they don't need. Her question is that she's trying to figure out what you'd go in the a la carte menu, the entree menu and the full meal. The first thing I thought when I saw that idea was I wasn't crazy about it. And for a couple of reasons. Cheryl I'd like you just to think about what message does a tiered fee structure send to prospective clients. If you can structure it in a way so that they're saying well I just want you to write an email blast. That is okay. But if it sends the message that well if you want good copy it's this much and if you want better copy it's that much. And if you want my very best then it's this much. If it sends that message even subliminally then you're working against yourself. The other consideration that I have on this is how does a tiered fee structure facilitate closing the deal. One of the biggest mistakes that copywriters make when they're creating promotions is that they give the prospect too many choices. And so the prospect delays making a decision and as a result eventually forgets about the deal or something else comes up and doesn't order at all. So any situation where you complicate your client's decision making process is going to you run the risk of having them say well jeez I better go talk to my marketing manager about that. Or I better talk to my wife or I better poll our employees to see how many of these services they really want. So to me this kind of it can unnecessarily complicate the entire deal making process between copywriters and clients. What I would recommend is and what works for me now what works for me won't necessarily work in all markets but all I can really do here is just share my thoughts on it with you and you can judge whether or not this might apply to your market. I just had to go through this with a client by the way and this is how we did it. First we created a credit rate card but we kept it to ourselves. We said okay well you know the client wants copy and Clayton isn't writing it and we're using a copy cub or a freelancer to help us with it. Then we're going to charge this much per hour. And we did that all the way down through all of the services we provide. At any rate here are the steps that I recommend. First decide how much money you want to make. Okay figure out what your goal is for the year what would make you really happy to earn this year. And then how much you need to make this month to hit that target. Then double that amount and divide by 160 which is the number of hours and work hours available to you in the month. That should yield an hourly rate. Now the reason you want to double it is because you're not going to be working 100% of those 160 hours writing copy. You're going to be spending some time selling. You're going to be spending some time addressing client crits. You're going to be taking some time off. So a double is a pretty good rule of thumb when coming up with this hourly rate calculation. Secondly I would identify the kinds of projects you want to work on. You want to do only promotional projects, list them. You know a direct mail piece, an email blast, a sales page, an online sales page, a print ad, a television spot. Whatever the kinds of projects you want to work on just make the list. Third calculate the amount of time these projects typically take you. An email blast takes me eight hours. And in word my average email blast runs four pages. So you divide the number of pages to arrive at your page rate. So if it takes eight hours multiply that time in your hourly rate and divide by the number of pages to arrive at a page rate. Fourth when asked what you charge for a particular type of job, you need some information. First ask them who's going to do the research on this. You know do you have the research all tied up in a nice little bundle? So ask who does the research. Are they going to deliver the research or are you going to have to spend a day, a week, two weeks doing the research yourself. That's a very real part of your cost structure and what you should pass on to your client in terms of each job. And if the answer is that you have to do the work, calculate how many hours you think it will take. And add 20%. I like 20% contingencies simply because I tend to be overly optimistic and I underestimate the amount of time the project will take me. And or the client will change things along the way in terms of the approach or a sales argument. Or they'll say well I found out that's not valid or my lawyer won't let me say that or whatever. So things come up and adding contingency rates really helps. And so multiply that times your hourly rate and come up with a number that you're going to have to charge for the research. Then find out how many pages the client expects. I'm a big one on not limiting the length of a promotion especially. But if the client is expecting a 24 page magologue and you deliver an 8 page letter, there's going to be trouble in paradise. Whether it makes sense or not, even though your 8 page or could outsell the magologue. But if the client's expectations are higher and you deliver less, it's not going to be pretty. If the client says oh I don't know, I see it being 16 page sales letter. I would double the client's page estimate. Again, that's your contingency. And that's in case the page count sometimes increases. The client may require more drafts than you expect at the outset. And so forth. So add a contingency. Fifth, multiply the page rate by the number of pages including your contingency. And then confidently look your client in the eye and say, well, for this kind of a job, I get X. I get $10,000 or $12,000 or whatever the number is. Now in many cases, especially with new clients, your client is going to feign shock and disbelief at your rate. Or at least try to negotiate in some way. Now if you've done everything the way that I've laid out for you here, you've got lots of wiggle room. You could offer to do it for less. But at the minimum amount of time that you think it might take. But have an allowance in the agreement for adjustments to your invoice if the page count increases or if more than three drafts are required. I do that with graphic artists all the time. In fact, I just had an invoice like that come in where we were planning on a 48 page book log and it turned out to 64 pages. And so, you know, we had agreed in advance that if the page count changes, he would be paid a certain page rate for the additional pages. You could offer to do it for less if the client provides research. In a lot of cases they'll do that because they have researchers on staff or freelance researchers who will do that work for less than you'd have to charge. See, you could offer to do it less, but ask for a royalty. And royalties typically will run anywhere from $10 to $50 a thousand in the mail or 10% of sales, whichever is more. On this one, you might even consider doing it with no advance and just say, look, you know, here's your choice. Jim Ruckst does this. You can pay me $500,000 cash now as a flat fee to write this package or I'll write it for free, but you have to pay me $50 a thousand. And a client will almost always go with a $50 a thousand royalty. Obviously, you should only do this if, A, you know and trust the client to be honest with you about how many pieces you mails or how much money the piece is making. And then, B, that you have a high expectation, a reasonable expectation of substantial sales or substantial mailings of the piece. And D, I really love this one. It ties into the theme that I've been hitting on in the total package for the last two weeks in terms of leveraging relationship, freelance relationships with clients into long-term partnerships. You could ask the client what other projects he's got going right now or coming up soon, and then you could offer him a package deal. So I tell you what, you know, okay, well, you know, I gave you my price and it seems to be out of your ballpark. If you've got another job for me in the next two weeks or the next month or if you'd like me to take over doing this for you every month, then I can sharpen my pencil on my regular price. And your rationale there is that, look, you know, if I have to go out there and pound the payment and recruit another client and go through another learning curve, that costs me money. So frankly, I'd rather just do another project for you. One of the things I often tell clients is the time that I'm worst suited to write your promo is when I start writing your promo. And the time I'm best suited to write a promo for you is after I've just delivered one because I know your product inside out, I've studied your market, I'm good to go. You know, I don't need to do any research, market research at least, I could dive right in. So there's good rationale there for offering a discount if the client signed you up for more than one project. So in seventh, be sure to formalize your agreement in writing. Most clients you work with are going to be fairly honest with you. And I've only found a couple of times when clients tried to short me on royalties and I caught them at it and things were made right. But the one thing that you can't recover from is honest misunderstandings or disagreements over what the terms originally were. So you don't need a lawyer for this. In most cases, a simple letter laying out the agreement you've come to should be plenty. If you have my six-figure copywriting business course, I've even included some copywriter contracts in there that you could replicate if you want something with a little more teeth in it. So those are my thoughts on that, Cheryl. I hope it helps. Next question. I'm now working on a couple of courses and reading the classics. I haven't selected an itch yet, although I've got a couple in mind. How will I know to begin seeking jobs? Is it just a feeling that I'll have knowing that I'm ready? Robert, I'm sorry, but I have two daughters and this sounds like the same kind of question they asked me about getting married eventually. Here's my advice to you. First, do select an itch. It doesn't have to be something you're going to be in for the rest of your life, but it needs to be something that you're excited about now. I started out just doing investment promotions, then I moved on to health, then I moved on to politics, then I moved on to other things. But you need to find an area that you want to focus in. And to do that, you should ask yourself, what am I passionate about? What subjects? Am I crazy about economics and investment? Do I love the challenge of writing for the personal finance market? How to live better for less kind of stuff? And Susie Orman kind of things. Would I rather write for newsletters or actual supplement products? Would I rather do catalogs? What is it that I'm really interested in? I think that guys may say, Heaven for me would be just writing promotions for car companies or car customizers or car product companies or maybe sports. Companies that are involved in some way in sports. So that's a huge consideration because whatever niche you select, you're going to be spending a lot of time reading and researching in that niche. So it needs to be a subject that you're interested in. What do you already know a lot about or would love to learn more about and see what niches are making other writers the richest? Sometimes I'm a big fan of the idea that you should die broke and that you should... And that work is to make money. It's not a charitable exercise. And so when my children come to me and say that, oh, well, I want to be a nurse or I want to be a missionary to Africa or I want to be something else like that, I just tell them, no, that's what you do in your spare time. You want to be a musician, you want to write the great American novel. You do that stuff in your spare time. You get an education and go to work in an area that's going to make you the most money, the quickest so you can retire the soonest and have more life left over to do the things that you love. So sometimes just choosing the niche that's going to make you the most money, the quickest, could be the best way to go for you. Second, study what's being done in that niche now. Become your prospect. Buy the products being offered by the companies in that niche and track what they send you. Third, begin creating promotions for real products in that niche. You know, you get a promotion in the mail for a particular product and you look at it, you immediately spot four or five things you could do that are stronger than what's being done in the promotion you're looking at. And, you know, same online. You go to a web page and the copy is terrible. It's awful. It doesn't even present the benefits of the product. Sit down. You know, maybe buy the product, I don't know, but at least sit down and start writing and write a better website. Add to your sample file. Even though there are self-imposed spec assignments, do it. And then show those assignments to prospective clients. Send it to the company whose webpage you just powered up or the company whose direct mail piece you just strengthened. And then give them a call and talk to them. And I've often talked about how I got my first newsletter promotion assignment when I had like, I think it was $60 in my pocket because I just returned to U-Haul Truck from moving to Arizona. And that was all the money I had in the world. And I was down in Phoenix returning the truck. So there was a publisher there, a research publication, and they had a newsletter called Daily News Digest. And I called up the publisher and I said, I just saw your direct mail piece and it really stinks. And I, you know, I can beat your response and I'll do it for free. But if I'm successful, then you have to pay me. And I named my price. And he said, well, you need to come over here and talk to me. And so just on the spur of the moment impulse to call the guy, I walked away with an assignment that became pretty much a career for the next two years. Because after I increased response on his first newsletter, Daily News Digest, he immediately retained me every single month to do a promotion for every other newsletter he had plus renewal campaigns, plus all of the other things that he needed copy for. So, you know, calling clients with ideas is a great way to go. Now, or prospective clients with ideas is a great way to go. And don't chicken out and use email. Email should only support personal conversation and, you know, telephone calls and actual face-to-face meetings when possible. Too many people are intimidated or uncomfortable picking up the phone and talking to a prospective client. And so they chicken out by using email. It's never as effective. And it doesn't begin the bonding process between you and the potential client. So my advice is shun email. Never do an email when you have a phone close by. And only use email to respond to prospective clients' emails. And then only when that makes more sense than making a phone call. I want to back up just a second before we move on. April wants to know, if you're entering a new market with no prior experience in it, how should that affect your price? April, I'll tell you, you know, I'm not out there in the market like you are right now. I'm pretty much booked up with clients that I've had for years. But I never worried about whether or not I had experience in a particular niche. What I did was I looked at what the top writers in that niche were earning. And then I said, you know what, if I give you a winner, that winner is just as valuable to you as a winner that Gary Bensavanga gives you or someone else. Therefore, I deserve to get paid the same amount. And, you know, if I don't give you a winner, then you won't have to pay as much because I work on royalties. So, you know, I don't think you should allow the fact that you're new to a niche affect what you charge for your copy. See, the key is that copywriting is salesmanship. And if you take a good salesman, in January you can tell him to go out and sell investment-grade diamonds. And in February you can tell him to go out and sell a new brand of toilet plungers. And in March you can tell him to go out and sell new cars. And he'll be equally good at all of those things because he understands human nature and he understands what needs to be said to make a sale and how to identify and overcome objections and those things. So, enough said. Basically, set your price based on what your financial objectives are and stick to it. And if a client won't, I gave you several fallback positions as I was answering question number one. If a client will not work with you on that level, you're probably better off to just say sayonara and move on. Go on to a client who's more reasonable because a client who won't work with you on that level is a client who doesn't understand and respect the power of good sales copy. And that means that you're going to have a tough time with that client on every project. So, hope that helps. Let's see here. What do we got? Winnie Anderson. I'm working at an ad agency now. And going out on my own soon, should I wait until my website is done and to pitch prospects? Absolutely not. Having a website up doesn't, not having a website up doesn't mean you don't have a telephone now or a way to jump in a car on a city bus and go see a potential prospect. The website is a nice garnishment. I don't know if any of you have noticed, but my agency response ain't, doesn't have a website. We're working on one and it'll be up fairly soon. But the work comes before all of that and networking comes before that. And, you know, do whatever you can every day to network, to find prospects, to talk to them, to share ideas, to prove your expertise, to let them see your portfolio, both actual assignments you've done and also spec assignments. And probably the most important thing you can do when you're talking to a prospect is give him an idea that he hasn't had before or failing that, a good idea that he hasn't had before, a failing that, give him an idea that confirms an idea that he already likes. And if you do those things and make those contacts often enough, you'll begin building your business in short order. Okay, John, hi John. John Gilger. Where do I find the phone number of the publisher? In the maft head of every newsletter and magazine, it's usually located in newsletters at the bottom on page two. And in magazines somewhere inside the first two or three pages of the magazine, there's a little box called the Maft Head. It lists the names of the major people at each, at that magazine. In the newsletter box, it'll list the marketing director. It might use a euphemism like customer care representative or something. But those will be the names and the phone number will typically be there. And the good news is the email address for the marketing manager in most cases probably will not be, so you'll be forced to pick up the phone and talk to them, which is really good. Lawton Child, the grandson of Governor Walken Lawton of Florida. We just met on our blog the other day. Lawton wants to know, is starting in a small town a huge mistake? No, it's not law. It can be somewhat of an obstacle. I mean, when I was getting started, I lived in LA, so there are a couple of things that I did. Number one, I sent a letter out to a whole bunch of people in a market niche I wanted to write for. And ended up with a couple of clients on that. But they lived an hour and a half away. I lived on the beach and they lived in Santa Monica or Beverly Hills. So even though I was in a big city, the travel time was fairly extensive. Wherever you are in Florida, pretty much, you're not going to be much farther than an hour and a half from a good-sized town. So I don't think that will be too much of a restriction for you. The other thing I did in LA was I contacted all the ad agencies in town and offered them my services as a copywriter when their copywriters went on vacation or when they were booked up. And I called my little company Copy Overload and I had one other writer working with me. And we ended up working for BBD&O and Smith & Hemings and other really huge ad agencies in LA. So that was being a really huge town that was helpful. But the bottom line is with the internet today and cell phones and so forth, I mean I've got a client in Washington, DC, LA that I've never met. These are just people that I've contacted or have contacted me and we wound up finding a way to work together. So I think maybe being in a small town will be more of a mental obstacle to you than it really would be for really finding clients. Because if you use the mail, you use email, you use the phone, you're persistent and you're helpful to your prospects and your contacts, I think you're going to do really well. Okay, question number three. I'm an ultra-competitive person who hates to lose at anything. This is from Mike and I think I would like Mike. I'll easily pull 40-50 hours on a client project trying to get the sales letter perfect for my client. I'll pay another copywriter to critique it for me before I ever turn it over to my client just to make sure the copy is rock solid. Yet I still haven't produced a big hit for clients with any consistency which frustrates me. What am I doing wrong? My ego is taking a serious beating. When I first read this, Mike, the first thing, well, as I said, the first thing I thought was I'd like Mike. And the second one was Mike's going to make it big time. And the third thing is that I thought to tell you was that you're not doing anything wrong. The more your ego gets beat up when you lose, the less cavalier you're going to be when you take on your next project. The more likely you're going to be to think it through in my new detail to avoid making whatever mistakes contributed to the last fiasco. I'm a big one for talking about my failures and I got to tell you when I first got started I went years, two or three years, without having a single significant winner. And I had to go through client after client after client where the expectations were high. I went in, wrote something that flopped and then deal with the client's disappointment. So I know how tough it is. But that toughening process, it's like how a samurai sword is made. They continually pound the middle back on itself, pull the middle back on itself and pound it until they get all the impurities out of the blade. The same thing is happening to you right now. And I know at times it feels like you'll never get beyond that phase, but trust me you will. Perseverance is the key. It also could help you might think about the fact that the full weight of the success of promotion does not rest on your shoulders. I just real quickly sat down and scribbled out 13 different reasons why promotions fail. The product could be ill-conceived. The pricing could be too low or too high. Sometimes there's only a certain response rate you're going to get. So you better ask for the right price, a high enough price. There are other times when your market for a particular product isn't going to pay more than a certain amount. And so you want to make sure that it's in the right range. The guarantee was weak. Clients are famous for chintzing out on the guarantees. And that's a real credibility killer in a promotion. The premiums stunk. I've had this happen time after time where I took an assignment from a publisher and he said, okay, I want you to write this package, but we're not going to create any new premiums for you. You have to use premiums that we've already created. And I looked through them and they were terrible. There was no content there. There was no value there. I would have had to lie in order to make them seem valuable to prospects. The list was poorly chosen. You have no control over this. Okay? But, you know, especially at smaller companies, you could go in and the guy just calls up a list broker someplace. And to the list broker, this guy is the smallest fish in his pond. He's not going to make much money renting this guy lists. And so he just wants to get him off the phone and get his check in. And so he doesn't take time to research the available mailing lists out there or email lists out there or other media if he's doing, say, paid advertising on the web or he's doing radio or TV. And so, you know, the list broker or the person who made the decision to rent the list could have been just totally out to lunch. And you could, you know, find out later on that you were trying to sell prostate relief to a list of women. So, and that's a huge one. I mean, when Bob Stone wrote successful direct marketing methods, he said that the list selection is 60% of the success of every promotion. So, you know, you start out wrong there. You're screwed from the get go. The promo wasn't delivered well. We've had this situation happen, you know, quite often over my career where we find out that the post office is dumping mail, you know. And it tends to happen, especially when there's a discontent among the workers or at peak mail seasons. And, you know, you'll read reports where they find that, you know, there's a whole bunch of bags dropped down, a mail drop down a sewer someplace. Major news events are in prospects lives. Oh, major news events or prospect life events. They press readership rates. I had this happen twice in 2007. In both cases, I should have known better because they weren't major news events as surprised as they were holidays that we knew were coming. But we timed our promotion too close to the holiday. You know, still, you know, our peak response period was designed to be over with a couple of days or three days prior to the holiday beginning. But what happened was, you know, in both cases, Thanksgiving and Christmas, people didn't start taking off work or leave town two days before. Because of the timing of the holidays, they were leaving on the Thursday prior to Christmas or on the Wednesday prior to Christmas, you know, nearly a week in advance. And so as a result, our response was cut short. And that happened to me twice last year. Another time, I can give you several other times when major news events have killed response in the mail. When Reagan was elected and we were selling investment newsletters, all of the rich old white men, the conservative Republicans who subscribed to financial newsletters, thought they'd elected the Messiah. And they didn't need advice anymore because everything was going to go up. And that was it. You know, response just died for like three or four months. Another time was the O.J. Simpson trials. And those things were on. Nobody was reading their mail. Another time was the beginning of both Iraqi wars. So, you know, anytime there's a huge news event, people read their mail less. They read their email less. They spend more time watching TV. And response suffers dramatically in some cases. I had a client who was putting, he had been, he had taken over a year to build up a big enough war chest to do a million piece mailing. He dropped it two weeks before the, what was it, 1980 elections, right? When Reagan was elected, I think it was 80. And then Reagan was elected and his response just stopped and hit a wall. So, the theme for the main headline and our opening copy failed a resume. This is a biggie because a lot of times, especially when you're working with larger direct mail companies, they will tell you the theme that they want you to write on. You know, if it's an investment promotion, they may say, well, we want this to be about inflation or the falling dollar or the crashing stock market or foreign stocks rising or foreign currencies rising or whatever. We want a package on that theme. Well, in those cases, the choice of the theme is not yours. And if you're up against other writers on the promotion and you get lucky and you get the theme that people are thinking about, worrying about and willing to pay for information on the most, then there's a good chance that even weaker copy is going to win. And don't think for a minute that those companies don't know that. They do know that. And that's why, you know, they're going to come back and hire you again if you did a good job with a copy regardless of whether you won or not. The promised benefits were not credible. You know, they had no testimonials. Their main product claim just wasn't believable. You know, there are no proof elements to speak of that they could provide you with. The closing didn't convince prospects to be to order now. This gets to the offer. And a lot of times you're restricted on what kind of offer you can make. Some clients don't want to use urgency premiums. You know, early bird bonuses and things like that. Or if they do, they give you very weak ones that just really don't help snag the sale immediately. Sometimes the order device is difficult to use. And, you know, that may or may not be late at your feet, but, you know, in most cases that's a graphic artist who really doesn't know how to make a response device simple and accessible. And so that's another reason. Sometimes, rarely, but sometimes there are technical disruptions in order flows. The BRE is coming back or there's, you know, phone lines go down, banks go down, servers crash. Not often, but it's something worth checking out. And then number 13 is that, you know, maybe you sucked. Maybe the copy sucked. The likelihood of that being the case is extremely remote. I mean, you've done your homework, you've studied copywriting, you know how to write a good promotion. You did your best on the project. You thought your way through it. And, you know, there's a 13 to one chance that it was something else or a combination of other things that really affect a response. Lottin wants to know, is Dunhill a good list company? Well, you know, Lottin, I haven't been involved in mailing lists for a long time, but when I was involved, the company, the brokerage I worked with, was a Dunhill franchise. And so I came to know the list pretty well. Their lists are compiled lists. Somebody went to public records or a phone book and, you know, artificially put those lists together. That's why they're so cheap. These are not people who have qualified themselves by buying something, anything in the niche area, or, and especially not necessarily buying anything through direct response. So avoid compiled lists. When you're looking at mailing lists or email lists, you shouldn't be interested primarily in demographic selects. You shouldn't care about the age of the sex or where the people live or any other facts about them. The only fact you should really care about is, has this person previously purchased a product like mine at the price that I'm charging for my product? Have they qualified themselves as being good prospects for my offer? And those lists don't cost $10 or $20 a thousand. Those lists of people who have purchased a product like yours at your price or near your price in the fairly recent past tend to go for substantially more. In the financial newsletter field, you know, they can go for $120 to $150 per thousand. In the health field, they can go for $100 to $120 or $30 a thousand because those are buyers. A compiled list is basically somebody went to the motor vehicle department and got their records and, and compiled a list of everyone with the word doctor before their name. And then they turn around and sell this as a list of doctors. You know, you don't even know if they're medical doctors or if they're PhDs in anthropology or, or physics. You know, they have a doctor in front of their names so they end up on the list. And they're certainly not qualified. So unless Dunhill has changed their stripes and started offering buyers lists, then my answer would be that unless you're doing occupant mailings or something like that, I would find a better broker. Okay, let's see here. Cheryl says, by the way, thanks for the advice on my question. I've been dragging my feet creating that thing. Now I can just whip something up and if they don't like it, I'll find more clients elsewhere. Good. I love that attitude. Always remember, you are the scarce resource. The lack of what you do is what keeps those companies from growing. And it keeps them from expanding their product lines. You have to have a sense of yourself for it. And the import, and the importance of the role that you play. I want to finish up this thing on Flops with another set of thoughts because this is something all of us deal with. Carly and I, and Glade Cole is famously revealed that whenever she has a bomb, she calls me up in tears and screams, I suck, I suck, I suck. And every single time it happens, I tell her the same thing. Grab your wallet, grab your purse, jump in that beautiful new Mercedes you drive, go to the mall and spend way more money than you can afford to spend. And then tomorrow come back and dive back into the work. But right now, you just need to, you need some healing time. You need to deal with your diminished expectations as a result of this failure. And then you need to come back and you need to figure out why it failed and learn your lessons. Okay, there are three kinds of Flops. And this could be of help to you when you have a flop. This could help you save the day. Okay. The promotion could have, for example, generated a low response rate but a high average sale. Okay. What does that mean? It means not many people read the copy. That, hence, the low response rate. But those who did read it bought like crazy. They were sold. Right. So the culprit in that case is what? It's the device used to convince people to read the copy in the first place. It's a headline in the opening copy. So the solution would be to go back to the client and say, look, and you know what? You'll be amazed at how few clients understand this stuff. Okay, what I'm telling you now. There are marketing departments, people who have degrees in marketing will sit there with their mouths hanging open. Why did I, as you explain this to them? Because they haven't heard it before. Low response means not many people read it. High average sale means the copy itself did this job, did really well. So all you have to do is go back to clients and explain that and say, so here's some new headline and opening ideas that I have. And let's give these a test. The clients already invested a bunch of money in paying you to write the thing in the first place. Why not drop another 20 or 40 or 60,000 pieces in the mail, two or three panels where you test headlines to see if you can't get that response rate up. You do that and you instantly turn this from a loser into a winner. The second kind of flop is just the reverse. It's good response but with low average sale. Okay, so plenty of people, the headline, the opening copy stopped plenty of people and convinced them to read the piece. But the sales copy wasn't compelling enough, didn't impart enough confidence to them for them to want to go with the highest purchase option. If it's a newsletter, for example, a two-year subscription rather than a one-year subscription. Or if it's a supplement product, a six-month supply instead of a three-month supply. So what do you look at then if your average sale was low? They read it, they just weren't convinced enough to go with the biggest purchase option. Here's your analysis. I just went through it. The culprits could be the product itself. I'd rather try it before I buy it. I'm not really sure if this is for me. The price, they might say, well, I can afford one year but two years, I'm stretching it. I have talked to my wife first and she laughed at me so I'm not going to do that. The premiums. You have premium structures that incentivize people to go with the higher purchase option. Maybe it's extra bonus reports or maybe it's some free supplements if they go with six months, they get the seventh month free or whatever. Look at the premiums. What can you do to boost the value of the premium structure for two-year buyers or for the buyers that go for the highest option? While you're doing that, also look at your copy on the premiums and make sure you did a darn good job of fully dimensionalizing the value of those two-year premiums. The offer itself, that is basically the guarantee, for example, making sure that two-year people are not punished for trusting you and going for two years. It could be weak benefit copy. Again, it gets back to, well, product sounds pretty good. I think I'll try it but I'm not fully convinced so I won't go with the biggest option. We credibility devices. It's so easy to grab a bunch of testimonials, write a headline, edit them so they make sense and are short enough that they'll be read, slapping a photograph on them and making sure that there are two or three of those on every spread and that every once in a while there will be an entire page or sidebar that's got a dozen or more testimonials on it. It doesn't take much to go out and, you know, one of the cool devices I like to use is I like to co-opt the names of companies that my prospects already trust. So if I'm writing a promotion for an investment product, right, and I want to say, well, you know, if you buy options on ETS, let's say options on natural resource ETS, Bloomberg says that if you had bought this option back on October 16th and spent $5,000 on it, seven days later you would have had $25,000. Bloomberg said that, you know, and it's true. I mean, Bloomberg has a research service. You go to Bloomberg, you look up the options chain, you look at what it did, and now you can attribute the data to Bloomberg and you can do that in charts, you can do it in graphs and tables as well as in the running text. But, you know, it's just, you know, no charge for that one. It's just a fun little way to bring additional credibility to your promotion. And the solution is obvious. Identify the areas you feel are weak and rewrite the affected sections. This, you know, you spent a month on a job doing this shouldn't take you more than a day or two, and it could save the whole project for you. It might even save the client some cases. The third one is low response and average sale. Now, analysis, you're screwed. Culprits, nothing worked. And at that point, rethink everything. If I get myself into a situation, I just look at the client and say, sorry, I can't help you with this one. I don't have a clue. You know, just be honest with them. They know it anyway. And by being honest with them, no respect, and you'll have a better shot at the next project with them. You know, I have a client, for example, that in fact, I think Chris might even be on the call. I have a client where we've had very low conversion rates on his online promotions. Chris has done a great job of producing tremendous flow of leads using pay-per-click and banner ads and ad sense and so forth. But the people just aren't converting up front. You know, eventually they'll probably buy some. But we haven't been making our money back up front. And the more I looked at his campaign strategy and thought about it, I thought, well, you know, we're kind of misleading these people. We're telling them, come on over for a free report. And then when they opt in, they give us their name and email address. They don't get a free report. They get a promotion piece for a product. And so now not only are they not buying the product, but I've got to piss off new subscribers in my free e-zine. This isn't total packages for a client's e-zine. And so, you know, we started thinking about that. Now, you could have written the best copy in the whole world on that sales page and, you know, not sold a blessed thing. And in fact, I think we did. But the strategy was wrong. And it sent the message that you can't trust what we tell you. Because we told you that you're going to get a free report instead of you getting a pitch. So that's just one example. There are thousands like it. But Tony, you're absolutely right. Thank you for that. Okay. Gary Ramos says, what about when a client does not have an established promotion? How do you determine if your copy is pulling at an acceptable level? Okay. Well, Gary, if he doesn't have an established promotion, I assume you're talking about a new customer acquisition promotion. And the client will have his own criteria based on his business model for whether or not your promotion worked. For example, in direct mail, the accepted model in the newsletter industry is that you want to generate a new customer and at the same time generate enough revenue to offset your mailing cost. Okay. In other words, break even or better. The amount of money that you collect from that promotion offsets your cost for printing, list rental, letter shop, and postage to send the promotion out. Okay. And that's the criteria most large newsletter publishers go by. They then pay for the fulfillment of the product by doing renewal at birth promotions and by selling back-end products, more expensive services typically, or in the health newsletter industry supplements to that new subscriber. So that's typically how it's done. So at any rate, he'll have a number in his head. If he's paying $560 a thousand to mail the piece, that means that he's got to get a response rate in average unit of sale that brings him $560 back. Okay. That gives you a range of response rates and average sale amount that you can hit and still get him that $560. Now, on a test mailing first time out, that's his minimal expectation. Hopefully what will happen is you'll give him an ROI return on his investment of not, you know, $560 would be a 100% ROI return on investment. Hopefully you'll get him $2,000. Okay. You know, 400% ROI. Okay. Because on the test mailing to reduce his risk, he's mailed the very best list he knows about. And when he starts rolling out and testing other lists, that ROI is going to start coming down real fast. So, you know, your goal is to get as high an ROI as you possibly can on that first one, because that's going to allow him to load up with more and more lists next time in order to get his ROI down to 100% or break even. And we're going to look at that a little bit more in a minute. Good stuff. April wants to know, wouldn't the phone survey of his best current clients give him concrete ideas rather than guesses to compile emotional reaction to benefits? Absolutely. But yeah, if he feels he needs that, I think that's a great way to go. But the fact of the matter is he's been doing this for a while. He's seen commercial realtors react emotionally to the various benefits that he provides to them. And so, you know, he's starting the process with a really good visceral understanding of what his benefits are and what both practical and emotional benefits are. If he still needs more, then sure, you know, take a client out to lunch, buy him a martini and get him talking. But, you know, the process here is basically to understand that every business has a product that they sell. Sometimes they call it a service. It's still a product. Every product has features. It has height, size, weight, color. It does certain things, and it does them a certain way. Every product has practical benefits. These are the reasons you give your wife for buying the product. The excuses that you make to yourself for buying the product. It'll make me thinner. It'll make me, you know, it'll make me healthier. It'll do this, that, or the other thing. And then there are the real reasons we buy them, which are the emotional reasons. And, you know, for a commercial realtor, it could be advancement. It could be prestige. It could be freedom from financial worry. It could be the ego boost of being number one. You know, it could be a ton of things. And you'll find those things as you talk to them or as you just do business in that space. So, all right. You better move on. All right. James has a challenge for me. He says, he's never seen a good answer to this question in any direct marketing text or course. It's this, if you're mailing promotions to a fixed audience, like investors, often do you mail them and when, how do you change your promotion? For example, if you had a universe of one million names, once you've mailed them, you're controlled, then what do you do? A new package next month or three months later or a re-mail of the original package six months later? I've never seen anyone talk about this in depth. Well, I'm going to try not to disappoint you, James. Here's what I came up with. Okay. A total mail cycle is typically six to nine weeks. Okay. That gives you the ability to do up to eight mailings per year. The first two to three weeks are taken after the mail date, okay, are taken up projecting what your return on investment will be. The client knows that on average, by day X, he has 50% of his total money back from that mailing. And typically that day ranges, depending on the promotion in the market, will range somewhere between two and three weeks, 14 and 21 days from the mail date. Okay. Broad rule of thumb, but it's usually in their someplace, right? At that point in time, historically, he's received 50% of his response. So at that point in time in this mailing, he is now ready to say, okay, well, these test panels won, these lists worked, you know, because he can project his final revenues and therefore ROI. Then he, and he knows what lists worked and he knows what headline won and he knows which format tests won and all of that. He then takes a couple to three weeks to prepare his rollout or continuation, I think is a more proper term, of the promotion. And during that period of time, he's doing test panels. Okay. He's having copywriters and artists create new headline panels and new offer tests and new price tests and maybe format tests. And, you know, maybe this mailing was a mag-a-log and you want to see how a book-a-log with the same copy would work. So all of that creative work is happening in that period of time. And this is a compressed schedule, but this is ideal. This is the way it should be. And we'll be talking more about this as part of the series of articles I'm doing for TTP. And then it takes a couple of two to, and also during this period, he's renting the mailing list he's going to use. And they're being delivered and then they go to a merge purge house. You know, he rents 20 lists. They're all in the same niche. There's going to be overlap. The same names are going to be on, you know, several of those lists. And there could be 20, 30, 40, up to 50% dupe rates between the lists. And so those duplicate names are removed, so he doesn't have to pay posters to send the same promotion to the same guy twice or three or four times. So that process has to happen during this period of time. Then he has two weeks to print, mail, letter shop, which address, sort, and drop, you know, put postage on and drop the mailing. So altogether that's anywhere from six to nine weeks depending on how efficient the mailer is. All right. Now some mailers will get more aggressive. Let's say, for example, your order processing can't handle 20 million, a 20 million piece mailing once every six to eight weeks. Or let's say you want more frequent mailing data than you get just mailing one, you know, one mailing after another sequentially. Right? Well, then you create what's called a bicycle. And you have several mailings all happening overlapping. Okay. Mailing one is here and you're projecting your ROI moving on to preparing your rollout and printing and mailing. While you're doing that, you know, you're this far along. Okay. Now you've got your second group of mailing lists and they're producing their own ROIs and suggesting their own tests and so forth. And so you're moving ahead with those then when the time is right. And then your third group of lists comes in and you're able to project ROI and you move that one and so on. So you can load up this bicycle as much as you want to and get your mailings really huge if you want to. And I've gone as high as, I think, three wheels at a time when we were being really aggressive in good mailing times. Okay. So I'm getting to the answer. I just wanted you to take you through that process. And here's what typically happens. You have a test mailing, right? The first answer is you want to mail your promotion to the same names every 68 weeks to your best names every six day weeks. That's the ideal, right? Here's what typically happens. Okay. Here's the ROI breakeven line. 100% that costs you $600 a thousand a mail. You get $600 back for every thousand pieces mailed. That's this response level here, right? Okay. So you go out with your test mailing, mailing number one. And it comes in way the heck up here. What does that look like? 220% of cost, right? So you've got a really great ROI. Everyone's all excited. We've got something we can mail, right? Then step two is the continuation. In this mailing here, you've mailed maybe 20, 40, 50, 100 lists, but you've only mailed a small representative sample of each list. Probably 20,000 names is my favorite because a number, it's my magic number because it produces statistically reliable results in most cases. There's a whole calculation thing that you can do there that has to do with the size of the total universe. And to come up with a number that you need to mail to develop or to come away with a reliable number, but 20,000 is a good rule of thumb, 20,000 per list. So mailing number one goes out and you're only mailing representative samples of each list. In those samples are selected on what's called an nth name select. The lists are sorted in geographic order by zip code. And then if there's 100,000 names, you want 20,000 names, you tell a computer to pick every fifth name. So you've got a good geographic sampling that's spread evenly across the country. So you mail those list fragments. Mailing number two comes along and you just did 222%. So the first thing you're going to want to do is a continuation. You're going to want to mail more of the names on the list that you tested that were successful, that did well for you. Now in this mailing, some lists pulled 600%. Some lists didn't even break even. So in here now, you're going to want to do continuations on all of the lists that broke, did break even or better for you. And then you're going to want, but in some cases if the lists are huge, you're not going to want to mail the entire list, the very next outing. You may want to be a little more cautious than that. Different mailers have different approaches. Me, if I get a good test here, I just go ballast to the wall here and do a full rollout. But a lot of people will test 20,000 names here, save a 100,000 name list. Maybe they'll test another 50,000 here and then they'll finish up on another mailing. But also in this mailing, you have some, at the same time you do this mailing, there's kind of another one that isn't shown here that has a whole bunch of test lists in it. And these are lists that, and I see you've proven your promotion here, right? So now you've got to prove mailing lists. So there's another mailing that happens simultaneously in here where you have a whole bunch of lists that have prospects on them that have very similar characteristics to the list that worked. But you haven't tested them yet, so you're going to go cautiously. So that happens here too, right? And then at some point you're going to get to the point where it's, you've mailed all of the names of all of these lists that have worked well with this promotion piece. And so now at some point you're going to get to where you're starting to repeat yourself and you're starting to go back and the same people are going to start seeing the same promotion piece. That's when attrition starts happening, okay? Obviously it could happen anywhere along and here for reasons we've talked about before, major events in the news, mail delivery problems, whatever. Or maybe the list broker just gave you the best names off the list and lied to you about it, which happens sometime. Of course those brokers don't stay in business long. But anyway, so now it's time to email to the same prospects, okay? And bang, you get yourself a nice little attrition rate. But you're still about break even. But this is a red flag that it's time to kind of get serious here and be thinking about what you can do to protect your ROI. And then the next email you're going to get even lower, okay? And that package attrition is predictable. You can pretty much look at history and know really and be able to see it happen and even predict it. You know, on a email, my average list attrits 20%. So if I got a $1,000 per 1,000 return, the first time I mailed it, I'm only going to get $800,000 the next time I mailed the same package the same file. So what I like to do is we do our test mailing. That proves that the package works and it tells us which lists among our favorite lists are going to work well for us. Then we have mailing two, which is a continuation. Immediately with that mailing, I want to test two to three new headline panels. And I may want to test some other things too. I may want to test price. I may want to test offer. When I say offer, I mean instead of just a one and a two-year offer on a newsletter, I may want to add a third-year option. Or I may want to add a continuity panel. I said, no money now. We'll bill you $30 a month for the rest of your life until you tell us to stop. So, you know, we put our tests in there. Now, on the very next mailing, that gives us some time now because this takes longer. I want to test alternate formats. If this is a MAGA log that we tested here, the first thing I'm going to want to say is how will that copy work in other formats? So I may do a six-by-nine package with a 16-page sales letter. I may turn the MAGA log copy into a book log, which is a six-by-nine thing that looks like a book. It's got a four-color cover like a book, and the insides are two colors. I may want to test a tablet. I may want to test a Slim Jim. I want to, you know, my options are unlimited there. And of course, what happens is, you know, you kind of get into a pattern here because over time you notice that if the safest piece to mail on the front end is a MAGA log, but if the MAGA log works, a book log is going to work better. Okay? And I found that, for example, in the health industry. And so I'm going to default to a book log test here. And I might add, I mean, here, and I might add another panel as well. Okay? And now, okay, now at this point, after this mailing is over with, what do I know? Well, I've got a new headline and opening copy that's kicking butt. It's kicking the original control all over the parking lot. And I've got a new format that gives me another lift. So, you know, now I'm going to combine the two, okay, and I'm going to go out in a promotion here with a new control. And it does get more complicated than this or can. I may, for example, want to test the original piece with my new headline and then test the alternate format, the book log or whatever, with my original headline and with, you know, a slightly different one, you know, that feels more book-a-log-ish, feels more like a book. So there are a lot of ways that you can play that. But your goal is to, with that second mailing, using the same copy, maybe just a different headline and different format to get your ROI back up here. And you repeat this process over and over and over again until the copy that you wrote originally, just no matter what you do to it, you're not able to get back up to your ROI targets. And when you don't get to your ROI targets, what happens? What that means is that the individual lists your mailing are each one of them performing less well. And at some point, some of them will stop hitting break even altogether. And when that happens, you can't mail them because your objective is to break even, right? And so what does that do? Well, it shrinks the size of your mailing. So the mailings start getting smaller. And when you see, you go a couple of mailings and you're unable to get a lift, and then you see your ROI falling, and the ROI on a lot of lists falling to the point where they're in danger of dipping below the break even level, it's time to get serious about writing a whole new, fresh package. Okay? So that's essentially the process that we go through. And the answer to the basic question is you want to hit every qualified prospect you can on every list that's doing break even or better as often as possible. And in the meantime, keep the promotion piece itself as fresh and as current as possible with whatever headline, copy, price, offer, test you can do, and also by changing up formats. Now, a lot of times what will happen is that you'll be a Magalog here and you'll test a Bookalog here, and lo and behold, they both work equally well. That's wonderful because now you can mail a Magalog here and then mail a Bookalog here and mail a Magalog here. And again, that's another way of slowing the attrition in ROI that would otherwise happen using the same copy over and over. So I hope that helps. Okay, Nathan is working with a client who's launching a new financial trading service in the UK. And if he wants to know, would I put together a complete selling message covering every possible benefit and plucking every possible emotional banjo string? I play a Stratocaster, Nathan. Or would you narrow the focus on a couple of key things for the first launch message and then create an extended launch period? This gives you a 40-page sales page or longer. This is basically doing a piecemeal. So another way to do a product launch, should I go for the juggler or give the prospect time to let his guard down first by a softer graduated introduction? The first thing, Nathan, is some of this depends on who you're going to. If you're going to your own customers, people who already know your messaging tone will just naturally tend to be a bit lower key anyway because you already have a relationship with them. Your friends, they trust you. They like your products. You like them because they pay you for them. So you write differently to friends than you do to strangers. So there may be some considerations there. But let me show you two different models that work. The first is a progressive promotion. You create a long copy sales page and you post it on the web. It has educational components, why you need to be worried about inflation now, why you need to be excited about gold prices going up, why you need to be in oil or whatever. And then presents every benefit of the product, all the credibility elements. This thing could be 40, 50, 60 pages long. It really doesn't matter because you're not going to be sending it out time after time after time to your prospects or customers. Then you go to your file and you do topical emails if this is for customers. And you say, oh wow, look at this, gold jumped $40 yesterday. It's really time to get on board. Let me show you the best way I know to make money in gold. Click here, bang, they go to the long copy sales page. You can do the same thing with direct mail, blog postings. I love doing live online events. We did one yesterday. We sold $1 million worth of financial services before midnight yesterday, between noon and midnight yesterday, off of a live online event that drove people to a long copy sales page. You can then follow by sending videos of that live event and a transcript of the live event to people, each one with a link on the page, thriving people to the long copy sales page. You can offer a discount tied to that, to watching that event and have a deadline for accepting it. And then do a countdown to the end of the discount. And then you can also do a countdown to the first trade that the service is going to issue. So basically this is kind of a progressive thing, right? And you can have a lot of fun with this. Now all of your communicating, this event is an hour long maybe, because it's content, content, content, with the last 10 minutes or so just being a pleasant chat with attendees about what the product does. This does your hard selling for you. So that's progressive promotion. A more linear promotion might go this way. You have invitation live event, countdown live event, live event, recording transcripts. And now this has to happen in particular order is my point, whereas the other one you can do in pretty much any order. And again, all driving to the long copy sales page. And I gave you some date things here and I know they look better on the PDF you got than they do here. So the answer is, you know, if you use long copy on all of your communications online to clients, then, you know, they're going to turn, you know, tune out and turn off after the first couple of contents, contacts. But that's okay because online is free. It doesn't cost you anything to send an email. Offline where you're paying five, six, seven hundred dollars to mail or, you know, per thousand to mail a direct mail piece, you can't afford to do a series. You can't afford to stretch it out. So for those kinds of promotions, you want long copy and you want to present every reason why the prospect should buy. Every single credibility element you can come up with and present a persuasive case. Understanding too that offline when you're doing direct mail, direct mail is not linear. Some people read Magalog's front to back. Some read them back to front. Some just let them flop open in the middle and start reading wherever their eye falls. People who are more methodical might start at the beginning but might scan through the pages, just kind of checking out the pictures and the headlines and subheads and then only reading body copy when it becomes, when something interests them. So it gives the prospect more freedom to jump around. It's much harder to do that on a long landing page online. So in the mail, you know, I'll probably go to my grave defending long copy. On the web, you have the luxury of multiple contacts for very little or no cost. And the best way to leverage that is to change it up constantly. Communicate through webinars, tele-seminars, videos, MP3s, podcasts, email blasts, you know, and landing pages. So change it up, take advantage of the medium. This question number seven is about handling drop-offs from the product or services delivered over a one-year period. They're selling a $995 product and they want to do a continuity program essentially. Okay, so you pay a 12 for that every month for 12 months. And Philip is where the potential drop-offs at certain points and he's wondering if you have any suggestions on how to maximize retention. Philip, here's a few ways to do it. I said four, they're actually five. Offer price and premium incentives to those who pay cash up front. You want to move as much of your people into the cash up front situation while also making the payment plan available to those who can't or are reluctant to pay the full amount up front. So, you know, that gives you the best of both worlds. You snag the most cash possible up front if you have good incentives for people who pay cash up front. And you don't have to worry about people dropping out of your continuity program because you've got their money. The only thing that can happen then is that they exercise a guarantee at some place. You're going to have to pay a refund. But, you know, if your people will do that, then we'll cancel a continuity program. You can offer continuity customers the opportunity to convert to annual once per quarter. This is called renewal at birth in the print newsletter industry, but I signed up for your continuity program. I'm going along fine. A quarter has passed now. So, you know, a fourth of my membership, the full membership now costs less than it would have had I bought it before because I've already made three payments on it, essentially. So, you can structure an offer for people to convert them at that point. You can over deliver with unadvertised surprise bonuses, which makes them want to hang around and see what you're going to give them next month. You can tease each month's benefits in advance. You know, be sure to be with us next month because here's what we're going to do together. And you can offer a big bonus at the end of each year. So, what I was just talking to, for example, has a coaching program and at the end of 12 months, if you're with him for the 12 months and you graduate from the program, you're invited to a live conference that other people are going to have to pay $2,500 to attend. So, you know, if you're only three months away from earning that perk and you really want to attend it and it's going to save you $2,500, you're much more likely to stick with the program. The answer to your main question is eight. For some reason, the eighth month on a continuity program tends to be the month in which most people see their biggest drop-offs. And also, there are other clients that have had who tell me that, you know, their overall average retention on continuity programs is right around eight months. So, it'll vary from industry to industry. But that's the point in time when the people have been there, done it, they've seen your product, they may have moved on, they may be less interested in whatever it is that you're delivering. They may have had negative results if it's a financial product. So, you know, eight seems to be the point in time when you really need to focus on retention. Any tips for increasing conversion on name squeeze pages? This is from Chris. These are my thoughts. And I want to preface this by saying that there's not a day goes by that I find that I don't learn something new about the web and find that some philosophy I've had and clung to is no longer valid. But here are the rules that we go by now. We try to keep them fairly short. We don't want them to be reading assignments. If the copy is longer, then that's fine. If it has to be longer, then that's fine. But keep it lively and absolutely don't go for length for length's sake. People are going to click a link. They're going to go to this landing page expecting a free report. The landing page is an interruption in their life because they were expecting, you know, click that link. Here's my free report. No, you're going to have to give us your name. So confirm what they'll get. Keep them short. Confirm what they'll get. You know, try to make. I like to try to make the entire process take as little time as possible. Establish your credibility somewhere on the page. Let them know who you are, what you've done, what others have said about you. One or two key testimonials might be enough. Demonstrate your privacy policy. I like to see a bug on that page that says something like safe unsubscribe or something like that, you know, so that I know that there's someone else looking out for me that all I have to do is click a link. And, you know, if you turn out to be a spammer, all I have to do is click a link that's at the bottom of every email and you disappear from my life. Demonstrate your cancellation policy. Oh, that was cancellation. I was just talking about privacy policies. Just demonstrate the fact that their email will never be shared with anybody. And basically demonstrate means, you know, have a policy or a bug that links to a policy window or something else that really elevates this to a solemn vow or if possible use a third party to guarantee somehow that, you know, names are protected. Show your business credentials. We're going through this long thing right now to get on Better Business Bureau preferred vendor status. It takes a lot of work, including in our case changing the domicile of our corporation because we're domicile then Florida but we operate at North Carolina. So it's worth it though, you know. You're a total stranger to people who see you online showing that you're a Better Business Bureau trusted vendor is going to go a long way towards making, providing their email address easier to do. Then do what you say you'll do. If the page says, you know, just give us your name and email address. You'll report to them, you know, to them immediately. Do that. Don't forward them to a page that is a report. You're saying that you need their email address because you're going to send their report by email or the link to their report by email. So do that. Add a viral element when they add their email address and they click send or enter. Have a script that pops up and says something like, you know, do you have friends or family that are also interested in the subject if so, enter their names here. We won't record them. Edit the email message below any way you like and click send. You know, be a hero to your friends and family. And then consider adding a force-free trial of some kind. You know, when people opt in on these pages, they're giving you their email address ostensibly to get a free report. They're also giving you permission to contact them and that has to be stated on the page. And we usually do that by offering a free easing. Okay. Well, you can also have a little pre-check checkbox. It says, please also give me three months of safe money report free. You know. And if I like what I see, I'll do nothing. And after my three months trial, you'll bill me for X dollars per year, X dollars per month or whatever, however you want to set up the deal. So, you know, it's worth a test. Having said all of this, I just want to point out that right now, recently we tested a new kind of squeeze page that actually begins the report discussion on the squeeze page that starts delivering some value on the squeeze page. And then says to continue reading, please, or to get the full report, please give us your email and your first name and email address. And my understanding is that, and I haven't really looked into it in detail, but my understanding is it's working quite well. So, we're going to be looking at that and probably playing with that idea as well. So, those are some ideas on squeeze pages. Do we recommend any German coffee writers? Are there any? Next question, what positioning tactics would you recommend for copywriters to take right now? Haroon wants to know this. I think it's less about positioning yourself as it is about selecting clients. If you read, and I know you did last week's issue or this Monday's issue of the total package, times are getting tough and it's companies that have products that can be perceived as being indispensable or helpful at a time like this are going to do quite well. And those are the companies that you want to be associated with, especially if you're on royalties. So keep in mind the environment we're moving into. Right now, for example, a coin company, a gold company, they're going to do quite well in this environment because all this money the Fed's pumping in is going to trigger higher inflation. We're already starting to see it. That's why gold is now selling at nearly $900 an ounce. The old All Time High was in 1980 and it was an 875 intraday, I think. So gold is taken off like a rocket. It's almost tripled in price in the last few years just because of people's concerns about inflation. Demand is up. People want coins and ingots as a safe hedge, as a safe refuge, safe haven. And so great business to be in at a time like this. We received on our blog, there was a guy in there, forget his name, but he has a car that will run all day or all week on $30 a month or whatever, $30 a gas, very fuel efficient and not very expensive. Anything that saves people money right now, anything that helps them ensure their job security, anything that I think we'll get to a question about that in a moment, about helping people when they lose their jobs. But be thinking about that. Many of my clients are not willing to spend money on bigger ads in the paper or to try to have ads look more like editorials, advertorial ads.
