A lot in my suggestion would be to go out and find a competitor or a near competitor who's making money hand over fist with these other media and go in and sit your client down and, you know, get as much intelligence as you can on them in terms of, you know, how many customers are bringing in. Look at the SRDS. Look at their hotline names. Tell you how many new names they're bringing in every 60 days. And if it's substantially more than your client's bringing in with his approach, you may be able to get some traction with that. And so, okay. But basically it's a sales job. You just have to reason with the client and provide enough proof to convince the client that it's worth a test. And, okay. Laun also wants to know what's the most effective way to track adverts in the paper and online. I see a little code, but how does that help? Okay. Very quickly, it's called a key code. This is how it's used in direct mail. This key code here tells you what mailing list was being mailed. Okay. And in this code are all kinds of little goodies like what the name of the list, you know, part of the code relates to the name of the list. Part of the code relates to any special selects on the list, like we may have just selected men or just women or just a particular zip code. But the marketing director who mailed this can take one look at this and tell you exactly based on this alphanumeric code, the list and the list segment, and list selects that were used. And then here we have the promotion key and this alphanumeric code tells you about this promotion here. Okay. So even, you know, if I call in, the operator is going to say, would you please give us your ID? It's on the back cover of the booklet you received. And he'll just read this number. Obviously the order form is on the reverse side of this back cover. So if they mail the order form, we also know we also capture this information. The promotion key here, this code can tell us whether it was a book log, mag-a-log, you know, a letter, whatever. It can tell us what headline was on it. It can tell us what the offer was. That's what the premiums were. These codes can be crafted anyway that you want to capture any information you need, right? So that's what those things are for. If it's something in a newspaper, the code will be much shorter than this because there aren't going to be all the permutations. There aren't going to be, you know, they're not going to be testing a whole bunch of headlines offers and prices and there's certainly not going to be doing list segmentation like you do in direct mail. So the code could be as simple as a little four-digit code that tells you that the ad ran in Barron's last, you know, in January. And then basically what happens is this information, when somebody orders, this information is entered on their customer record. So for time immemorial, we can go back and we know where that guy came from, what he responded to, and we can track how well buyers from that source perform long-term compared to buyers from other sources and so forth. So, crash course and key codes. Okay, these are from Mark. In the past, corporate retrenching has led to discharge employees to pursue new career paths with less dependency on corporate jobs. He then asks four questions. Do you see this happening again now? Answers yes. The last time around, I forget the number. I think it was something like 20 million new home-based businesses started after 9-11. Some huge, ridiculous number. And I definitely see it happening again, partially from people who have been fired and are looking for a way to replace that income. Partially from people who are afraid of being fired and want to have a fallback position. And partially from people who are just worried about inflation and want to add income. So they'll do it in their part time. So yes, I see that as being a huge opportunity in this recession. Do you think these newly-mitted entrepreneurs are a good market for info products? Absolutely. If there's anything they need, it's a roadmap to getting to where they want to go. So yes, again, what markets, categories, or other areas do you believe will yield the greatest returns? Where's the hungriest crowd? Well, I think you're all hungry. I think one of the things that you need to look at is the kinds of people that are likely to be laid off. And if you're looking at blue-collar people, then I think online marketing is a huge topic. How to get rich on eBay, how to run a successful eBay business, for example, or online auction business. Anything along those lines. Remember, though, the people who are getting laid off are not independently wealthy. The largest number of people who are going to lose their jobs in this recession or who are concerned about losing their jobs in this recession make a good, decent living. But they're not rich. And so, you know, they're not going to be able to invest 100,000 or 200,000 or 500,000 to start a business. They're going to want something that they can do fairly quickly with simple tools in their own home and then build it from there. So, you know, that's definitely a consideration. Personal finances are always good. In a recession, health is okay too. You know, I mean, we still all have bodies and hearts and we're all still fat and bald and we all want to make improvements. Yeah, Jonathan just said best market now, realtors. You know, Paris, not Paris. Yeah, it was Paris, got started. During the real estate recession, I think it was in the 80s. And he was in real estate and he wrote a book, How to Sell a House in a Down Market. And he sold a whole bunch of them, but he didn't understand direct marketing at the time. He had no follow-up product and even though his promotions were successful, he lost his money. So, you know, keep that in mind. But yeah, the real estate is a great, great place to go right now. Not helping people get into real estate with people, but helping people who are in it move properties, sell houses, find prospects, that kind of thing. And if you put your mind to it and think recession, think about the current economic environment and how things might go from here, you'll come up with others. I think retirees are going to be more and more pinched as cost of living continues to rise. They're on fixed incomes. And so every time the cost of living rises, it's not just that things cost more. It means they can't buy as much. They can't live as well. So, you know, anything that you can do to help those people would be great. Is there any special or unique way to connect with these potential entrepreneurs? Well, the obvious way I think really is online search. People are going to come to the web and they're going to type in home-based business and they're going to type in self-employment and they're going to, you know, entrepreneurship and, you know, those kinds of search phrases. And if you have a pay-per-click at the top of the search page or if you have a highly rated sales page, it's been optimized for the search engines. And you appear in the first page or two, you're going to get traffic from it. Another way would be to go to sites that cater to entrepreneurs and opportunity seekers and look at easing sponsorships where you do a deal and you say, okay, here's some money. Now, you're going to put an ad in your easing from my website that shows people how to do XYZ. And in addition, you're going to blast the file once or twice. You know, I mean, ArchaMax, Newsmax are great sites for that kind of thing. They put these kinds of amazing deals together for $2,000 or $3,000 on ArchaMax, for example. You can have an email that you write about your product or service blasted to 1.6 million people. And there are a bunch of residual benefits that go along with it as well. So, and also, I would like to suggest that you send an email to Julie McManus at our blog because her specialty is driving traffic through paid campaigns and she'll have a whole bunch of other ideas for you. So, hope that helps. Regarding customer acquisition, is there a place for giving away for free front ends to acquire a new name? Absolutely. That's what we do all the time on the web. We also, there are all kinds of permutations of this, Phillip. You know, one is a 60-day free trial of a product, which is essentially a soft offer, and you build them after 60 days. Another way to do it is to ask people to send for your free Magalog, which essentially is an advertorial that delivers value but also sells product. Online, though, online is where freebies rule. And looking around the web, you'll see it all the time. The second part of your question is about lost leaders. And yes, we, for example, are very happy to spend a bunch of money on pay-per-click or paid campaigns so long as we can, so long as at the end of 60 or 90 or even 120 days, we have our money back that we spent because those people have bought stuff from us. But we also have, you know, thousands of new names. So very valid, absolutely. But test your way into it because the key is how long it's going to take to get your money back. And you're going to find that some sources are going, you're never going to get your money back from because people they send you are going to be just absolute deadbeats. You're going to find that other sources are really great. So you need to be cautious and kind of test your way into it just because of this lag. Okay? Hope that helps. Here are some times to consider multi-step offline campaigns. Okay? This would be, you know, produce a lead by offering something free, then follow up with a sales piece or a call or something. So these are times that you might want to consider that. When your product price is huge, when you need to call prospects from a large universe cheaply before presenting your full sales message, let's say your full sales message comes in a $2 per piece presentation package. Maybe you're selling commercial loans or something like that. Okay? You don't want to send that to the phone book. You don't want to send it to a bunch of people who may or may not be in the market for a loan right now. So you can send a postcard to a huge file very cheaply or, you know, as part of an insert program or something like that and get lots and lots of prospects. And, I mean, go out to lots and lots of prospects. But then what happens is only the people that are interested at some level raise their hand and say, hey, this is something I'm interested in and I'm qualified and I think I could do this. Why don't you tell me more? And then you can go ahead and send your DVD or your direct mail piece or make a phone call to the person or do whatever is necessary to move them along the buying process. Sometimes a live sales person is required to answer questions and address objections. Sometimes closing a sale will require more than a simple order form. You may need to take them through a big long application of some kind and that may require a live help. And in some cases the product is infinitely customizable. I'm not talking about just colors and size here like with clothes. I'm talking about if you're selling plant equipment or office equipment or some of the thing that requires you to ascertain information about the prospect. You know, prior to advising them on which version of the product or which options to buy. So those are just some of the ideas, some of the reasons why people use two-step programs. Two-step programs just in terms of cost outlays are by their very nature more expensive than one-step programs because each step costs you money to send. And of course the first step doesn't result in a sale. So a good example though was there was this fantastic Smith and Hemmings promotion I saw when I lived in LA. And they were trying to sell commercial loans. And so you know, you've got this little tiny postcard, you know, you're a business person and you get this small postcard and it says, it wasn't a postcard, it was a small envelope, a little tiny thing. And it said free golf ball enclosed. You know, of course it's a flat envelope. So everyone, ha ha ha, and they opened it, right? The next week you got a little bit bigger and it said free tennis ball enclosed. And every week for the next like seven or eight or nine weeks, the ball got bigger. And finally at the end of the process you got this big thing with a life-size picture of a basketball on it. And in each communication there was a message driving people to the loan officers. And they could afford to do that because they had to do it because something like that, because competition was so high for commercial loans. So they needed visibility, they needed frequency in the mailbox. And they could afford to do it because the amount of money a bank makes on a, you know, multimillion-dollar business loan is more than enough to offset the cost of that kind of campaign. So those are some of the considerations to keep in mind. Times to consider multi-step campaigns online, always, they're free. Absence does not make the heart grow fonder online. The more that you can contact customers and deliver content, a value in those contacts, the tighter the relationship you're going to have with that customer. When it comes to a premium offer summary page, the sort of thing we put in acquisition promo right before the response device, what are your thoughts on the pros and cons of showing all of your, just your best offer versus all three options? Okay. Well, just so you know what Andrew's talking about, this is an inside-back cover. It faces the order form on a health resources promotion. And you'll notice on here, it shows all of this great free stuff you get, $267 in free gifts and discounts. Nowhere on this page does it mention that you have to go with the largest order, which I forget what it was. I think it was a six-month supply or something. The largest order amount to receive all of this, and that it was a graduated offer, so that progressively as you order the next two echelons down, you've got fewer freebies and discounts. But in this, when you get extra bottles of the main product for ordering, and you get this extra supplement with your minerals that complement the main product for, and you get four bottles if you get the select the highest order amount, plus you get these three free reports. Right? So my philosophy is put your best foot forward, explain the details on the order form. And also, you explain the details in the running copy, the offer copy and the promotion piece. But on this page, where you're trying to put your best foot forward, you want to show everything. You want to overwhelm them with the value of the offer, and you want to push them towards your best offer. Okay, Jonathan says, speaking of the real estate market, as an investor in commercial properties myself, I've noticed many vacancies with fellow owners' properties in the last three, six months. All these vacancies stem from the real estate contraction, real estate loan companies, escrow companies and mortgage brokers. Yeah, all of the above are great targets right now in this market. You know, basically, it's about desperation, isn't it? It's about the intensity of the fear or the intensity of the desire to either the fear of succumbing to a particular problem or the desire to overcome it. And so, just develop a nose for fear. Look for the people to whom your service will have the greatest value, to whom your marketing ideas and your copy are in most dire need. Those are the people who will be the most willing to partner with you and offer you great incentives, including a piece of the action to work with them. Jonathan also says, always test, always talking about the freebie lead, the loss leader offer. He says, offering free reports, start with a free report and then continue after the email is submitted, also offer a benefit for the contact. Right, absolutely. All right, I think we pretty much covered it all. Wow, you guys are great. Have a great rest of the day. Goodbye.
